questions.question: Explain the difference between memory READ and memory WRITE operations with reference to the direction of data flow.
keyPoints: READ: Data flows from memory to CPU (memory is source, CPU is destination)
WRITE: Data flows from CPU to memory (CPU is source, memory is destination)
Both operations require address to be placed on address bus
Control signals (READ/WRITE) indicate the type of operation
Data bus carries data in the appropriate direction

questions.question: What is the function of the Stack Pointer (SP) in memory operations? Explain with the help of PUSH and POP operations.
keyPoints: SP points to the current top of the stack in memory
PUSH operation: Decrements SP, then stores data at address pointed by SP
POP operation: Retrieves data from address pointed by SP, then increments SP
Stack grows either upward or downward in memory depending on architecture
SP is automatically updated by PUSH/POP instructions

questions.question: Differentiate between big-endian and little-endian byte ordering with an example.
keyPoints: Big-endian: Most significant byte stored at lowest memory address
Little-endian: Least significant byte stored at lowest memory address
Example: 32-bit value 0x12345678 at address 100
Big-endian: 12 at 100, 34 at 101, 56 at 102, 78 at 103
Little-endian: 78 at 100, 56 at 101, 34 at 102, 12 at 103
Each has advantages for different operations

questions.question: What is DMA (Direct Memory Access)? How does it improve system performance?
keyPoints: DMA allows peripheral devices to transfer data directly to/from memory
DMA controller manages the transfer without CPU intervention
CPU is free to execute other instructions during DMA transfer
Reduces CPU overhead for bulk data transfers
Commonly used for disk I/O, audio/video streaming, network cards

questions.question: Explain the concept of memory-mapped I/O and its advantages.
keyPoints: Memory-mapped I/O uses the same address space for memory and I/O devices
Device registers are mapped to specific memory addresses
Standard memory instructions (LOAD/STORE) can access I/O devices
No special I/O instructions needed
Simplifies CPU design and programming

questions.question: What is a memory access cycle? List the steps involved in a typical memory READ operation.
keyPoints: Memory access cycle is the complete sequence for one memory operation
Steps: 1) CPU loads address into MAR
2) Address placed on address bus
3) READ control signal asserted
4) Memory reads address and accesses data
5) Data placed on data bus
6) Data loaded into MDR
7) Data transferred to destination register

questions.question: Explain the difference between cache hit and cache miss. How does each affect memory access time?
keyPoints: Cache hit: Requested data found in cache memory
Cache miss: Requested data not in cache, requires main memory access
Cache hit: Very fast access (few CPU cycles)
Cache miss: Slower - must fetch from main memory (hundreds of cycles)
Cache miss also involves loading the data into cache for future access

questions.question: With the help of a diagram, explain the internal organization of memory and the process of reading data from a memory location. Include the role of MAR, MDR, and address/data buses.
keyPoints: Memory consists of addressable locations each storing a word
MAR holds the address of location to access
MDR holds the data being transferred
Process: CPU loads address into MAR → Address placed on address bus → READ signal sent → Memory reads data → Data placed on data bus → Data loaded into MDR → MDR data used by CPU
Address bus carries addresses from CPU to memory (one-way)
Data bus carries actual data between CPU and memory (bidirectional)
Control bus carries read/write signals

questions.question: Discuss the various addressing modes used in memory operations. Give examples of each.
keyPoints: Direct Addressing: Address specified explicitly in instruction (e.g., LOAD A, 1000h)
Indirect Addressing: Address stored in a register (e.g., LOAD A, [BX])
Register Indirect: Register holds memory address (e.g., LOAD A, [R1])
Indexed Addressing: Address = Base + Index (e.g., LOAD A, 1000h[SI])
Relative Addressing: Address = PC + Offset (for branches)
Immediate: Data value specified in instruction itself
Base-Register: Address = Base register + offset

questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual memory. How does it allow programs to use more memory than physically available?
keyPoints: Virtual memory creates illusion of larger contiguous memory space
Physical memory (RAM) divided into frames, virtual memory into pages
Memory Management Unit (MMU) translates virtual addresses to physical addresses
Pages not currently in RAM are stored on disk (swap space)
Page fault occurs when needed page not in memory - OS loads it from disk
Allows multiprogramming and larger programs than physical RAM
Provides memory protection between processes

questions.question: Explain the differences between write-through and write-back cache policies. Discuss their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Write-through: Write updates both cache and main memory simultaneously
Write-back: Write updates only cache initially, writes to memory later (when block replaced)
Write-through advantages: Simple, consistent, no data loss on crash
Write-through disadvantages: Slower (must write to slow memory every time)
Write-back advantages: Faster for repeated writes to same location
Write-back disadvantages: Potential data inconsistency, more complex implementation
Write-back requires dirty bit to track modified cache lines
